source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
A car with a mass of $m=1.0 \times 10^{3}\mathrm{kg}$ is traveling at a constant velocity of $v_{1}=36\mathrm{km/h}$ on a horizontal road. When the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the crosswalk, the driver observes a line of children, of length $l=6\mathrm{m}$, beginning to cross the road from one end of the crosswalk. The driver immediately applies the brakes, and the car comes to a complete stop just before the crosswalk. Assume that the frictional force acting on the car during braking is constant and neglect the driver's reaction time.

1. Determine the time it takes for the car to come to a stop from the moment braking begins.
2. Calculate the magnitude of the resistance force acting on the car from the moment braking begins until the car comes to a complete stop.
3. Given that the width of the road is $L=6\mathrm{m}$ and the children are walking at a velocity of $v_{0}=0.5\mathrm{m/s}$, calculate the time the car must wait before the crosswalk for all the children to pass completely.
4. Assume the driver is speeding at a speed of $v_{2}=54\mathrm{km/h}$ and applies the brakes when the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the pedestrian crossing, calculate the speed of the car when it reaches the pedestrian crossing.

image_dependency: All quantities needed for solving are stated explicitly in the text
multi_step: Requires kinematics and dynamics reasoning across multiple quantities: stopping time, braking force, pedestrian-clearance wait time, and residual speed
Sub-parts involve combining given values with equations rather than direct lookup
question_char_length=1264 suggests a substantial multi-condition word problem
verifiability: Each sub-question asks for a determinate physical quantity that can be recomputed from the givens
Provided answers include concrete values and units/radical form, enabling rule-based or symbolic checking

decision_reasons: Question text and answer are both present with substantial length, so core semantic assets are intact
Image dependency is negligible and all required quantities are stated explicitly in the text
High multi-step and verifiability scores indicate strong value as a reasoning sample
Risk flags are limited to answer formatting and unit variation, which do not undermine recoverability or solvability

3. Given that the width of the road is $L=6\mathrm{m}$ and the children are walking at a velocity of $v_{0}=0.5\mathrm{m/s}$, calculate the time the car must wait before the crosswalk for all the children to pass completely.
4. Assume the driver is speeding at a speed of $v_{2}=54\mathrm{km/h}$ and applies the brakes when the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the pedestrian crossing, calculate the speed of the car when it reaches the pedestrian crossing.

variants.title: PhysReason 开放题
variants.rewritten_question_text:
A car with a mass of $m=1.0 \times 10^{3}\mathrm{kg}$ is traveling at a constant velocity of $v_{1}=36\mathrm{km/h}$ on a horizontal road. When the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the crosswalk, the driver observes a line of children, of length $l=6\mathrm{m}$, beginning to cross the road from one end of the crosswalk. The driver immediately applies the brakes, and the car comes to a complete stop just before the crosswalk. Assume that the frictional force acting on the car during braking is constant and neglect the driver's reaction time.

1. Determine the time it takes for the car to come to a stop from the moment braking begins.
2. Calculate the magnitude of the resistance force acting on the car from the moment braking begins until the car comes to a complete stop.
3. Given that the width of the road is $L=6\mathrm{m}$ and the children are walking at a velocity of $v_{0}=0.5\mathrm{m/s}$, calculate the time the car must wait before the crosswalk for all the children to pass completely.
4. Assume the driver is speeding at a speed of $v_{2}=54\mathrm{km/h}$ and applies the brakes when the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the pedestrian crossing, calculate the speed of the car when it reaches the pedestrian crossing.

The driver immediately applies the brakes, and the car comes to a complete stop just before the crosswalk. Assume that the frictional force acting on the car during braking is constant and neglect the driver's reaction time.

1. Determine the time it takes for the car to come to a stop from the moment braking begins.
2. Calculate the magnitude of the resistance force acting on the car from the moment braking begins until the car comes to a complete stop.
3. Given that the width of the road is $L=6\mathrm{m}$ and the children are walking at a velocity of $v_{0}=0.5\mathrm{m/s}$, calculate the time the car must wait before the crosswalk for all the children to pass completely.
4. Assume the driver is speeding at a speed of $v_{2}=54\mathrm{km/h}$ and applies the brakes when the car is at a distance of $s=20\mathrm{m}$ from the pedestrian crossing, calculate the speed of the car when it reaches the pedestrian crossing.
